title: Trees
description: Binary trees are among the most tested interview topics. In Rust, TreeNode uses Option<Box<TreeNode>> for children. Master the node definition, DFS traversals (inorder/preorder/postorder), BFS with VecDeque, and recursive depth calculation.

sections.title: TreeNode definition and construction
sections.explanation: In Rust interviews, define TreeNode as a struct with `val: i32`, `left: Option<Box<TreeNode>>`, and `right: Option<Box<TreeNode>>`. Use `Box` for heap allocation (since recursive types need indirection). Build trees by assigning `Some(Box::new(TreeNode { ... }))` to children.

exercise.prompt: Define a TreeNode struct. Build a tree with root=10, left=5, right=15. Print all three values.

validation.message: Tree should have root=10, left=5, right=15
Pass 5 and 15 to TreeNode::new() and use Option<Box<TreeNode>> for children

exercise.hint: Pass 5 and 15 as arguments to TreeNode::new() inside Box::new().

sections.title: DFS traversals (inorder, preorder, postorder)
sections.explanation: **Inorder** (left, root, right) gives sorted output for BSTs. **Preorder** (root, left, right) is for serialization. **Postorder** (left, right, root) is for bottom-up computation. In Rust, pass `&Option<Box<TreeNode>>` and match on `Some(node)` / `None` as the base case.

exercise.prompt: Implement inorder DFS traversal. Build a BST: root=4, left=2, right=6. Run inorder and print the result space-separated (should be sorted: 2 4 6).

validation.message: Inorder traversal of this BST produces sorted order: 2 4 6
Recurse on &n.left, push n.val, then recurse on &n.right for inorder

exercise.hint: Push n.val between the left and right recursive calls for inorder traversal.

sections.title: BFS level-order traversal
sections.explanation: Level-order traversal uses a **VecDeque** to process nodes breadth-first. Since Rust's ownership makes tree BFS slightly tricky, we often work with references. Push the root, then loop: pop from front, process, push children. Use `.len()` per level for grouped output.

exercise.prompt: Implement BFS level-order traversal. Build a tree: root=1, left=2 (children 4,5), right=3. Print each level on its own line.

validation.message: Pop from front for FIFO, push left and right children for the next level

exercise.hint: Access the right child with node.right and push it to the queue.

sections.title: Tree depth (recursive)
sections.explanation: Maximum depth is the number of nodes on the longest root-to-leaf path. The classic formula: `1 + max(depth(left), depth(right))` with base case 0 for None. In Rust, pattern match on `&Option<Box<TreeNode>>` — return 0 for None, recurse for Some.

exercise.prompt: Implement a recursive max_depth function. Build a tree: root=1, left=2 (left child=4, which has left child=8), right=3. Print the max depth (should be 4).

validation.message: Base case returns 0, recurse on both children and take the max + 1

exercise.hint: Recurse on &n.right for the right subtree depth.
